perturbation.original_text: Astellas shall make quarterly reports to FG within sixty (60) days after the end of each calendar quarter (April 1 though June 30, July 1 through September 30, October 1 though December 31, January 1 through March 31), which reports shall include, (a) the Net Sales, unit shipments and other distributions, including samples, by Astellas, and its Affiliates and Sublicensees, in such calendar quarter and (b) such other information as may be reasonably requested by FG to ensure either proper payment by Astellas of amounts required under this Agreement or to calculate payments with respect to FG's Third Party Agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: Astellas shall make quarterly reports to FG within one hundred twenty (120) days after the end of each calendar quarter (April 1 though June 30, July 1 through September 30, October 1 though December 31, January 1 through March 31), which reports shall include, (a) the Net Sales, unit shipments and other distributions, including samples, by Astellas, and its Affiliates and Sublicensees, in such calendar quarter and (b) such other information as may be reasonably requested by FG to ensure either proper payment by Astellas of amounts required under this Agreement or to calculate payments with respect to FG's Third Party Agreements.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the reporting deadline from 60 days to 120 days could potentially cause issues if this contract falls under regulations requiring more prompt financial reporting.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act mandates specific deadlines for financial reporting to ensure transparency and prevent fraud. Extending the reporting period to 120 days may not comply with these regulations and could be deemed a violation if it impairs accurate and timely financial oversight.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the deadline for quarterly reports from 60 to 120 days. The Law Explanation states that the Sarbanes-Oxley Act mandates specific deadlines for financial reporting. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 7241, discusses corporate responsibility for financial reports and the regulations required by the Commission for companies filing periodic reports. The Perturbation Explanation notes that a longer reporting deadline may cause issues under regulations requiring prompt financial reporting. While Snippet 1 does not specify the exact deadline, it confirms the existence of regulations concerning financial reporting deadlines, and the extended deadline directly contradicts the implied need for timely reporting under these regulations.

perturbation.original_text: Such books and records shall be kept for at least [ * ] following the end of the calendar quarter to which they pertain. FG shall keep, and require its Sublicensee(s) to keep, complete, true and accurate books of accounts and records for the purpose of verifying the accuracy of the [ * ] Percentage and Fully Burdened Costs.
perturbation.changed_text: Such books and records shall be kept for at least one (1) year following the end of the calendar quarter to which they pertain. FG shall keep, and require its Sublicensee(s) to keep, complete, true and accurate books of accounts and records for the purpose of verifying the accuracy of the [ * ] Percentage and Fully Burdened Costs.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record-keeping duration to one year may conflict with legal and regulatory requirements for maintaining financial records, especially those related to tax and compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: U.S. tax laws generally require businesses to keep records for at least three years, with some records needing to be kept indefinitely. A one-year retention period does not satisfy those requirements and could incur penalties.

Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns Every person liable for any tax imposed by this title, or for the collection thereof, shall keep such records, render such statements, make such
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the record-keeping duration to one year, which directly contradicts the general requirement of U.S. tax laws, as explained by the LLM, that businesses keep records for at least three years. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 26 USC 6001, which mandates keeping records and statements as required by tax regulations. This aligns with the Law Explanation's point that a one-year retention period fails to satisfy those requirements, potentially leading to penalties. This constitutes an inconsistency leading to a legal contradiction because the altered period violates federal law.

perturbation.original_text: Any payments due under this Agreement which are not paid by the date such payments are due under this Agreement shall bear interest to the extent permitted by applicable law at the U.S. prime rate per annum quoted in the "Money Rates" column of The Wall Street Journal (U.S., Western Edition) on the first business day after such payment is due, plus an additional [ * ], calculated on the number of days such payment is delinquent.
perturbation.changed_text: Any payments due under this Agreement which are not paid by the date such payments are due under this Agreement shall bear interest to the extent permitted by applicable law at a rate of 25% per annum, calculated on the number of days such payment is delinquent.
perturbation.explanation: Setting the interest rate for late payments at 25% per annum is likely to violate state usury laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's usury law limits the maximum interest rate for loans and forbearance of money. A 25% annual interest rate generally exceeds this limit, especially if the lender is not a specifically exempted entity, making the contractual term unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The modified text sets a fixed interest rate of 25% per annum for late payments. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this violates usury laws, particularly in California. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing the California Constitution, states the interest rate on loans or forbearance shall be 7% per annum, but allows for written contracts to specify higher rates, up to 10% for personal/family/household purposes, or a floating rate tied to the Federal Reserve discount rate plus 5% for other uses. The 25% rate specified in the modified text likely exceeds these limits, creating a legal contradiction, unless a specific exemption applies that is not mentioned.
